rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. KINK.COM ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, Llc'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (Set Two)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday September 26, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT CYBERNET ENTERTAINMENT, LLC Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, Llc'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (Set Two) A key issue in this case is whether plaintiff's participation in the May 3, 2013 filming of a sex video was a cause of his HIV infection. Asserting that it is highly probable he was infected from April 20 to May 13, 2013, plaintiff has provided a list of his sexual partners in that time frame and the partners' HIV test results. Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, LLC, however, seeks to further compel discovery of such information from four months before and six weeks after May 3, 2013. Cybernet says, inter alia, that plaintiff's sex partners in those time periods "may be potential sources of HIV exposure." Plaintiff contends that Cybernet's discovery would be irrelevant and invade privacy. The "window of exposure" for HIV infection is within the ken of medical experts. Plaintiff relies on hearsay, including a doctor's letter. Cybernet adduces no on-point evidence. Both parties may submit expert declarations on this issue under penalty of perjury. This shall be done on or before October 5, 2018. Hearing of the pending discovery motion is CONTINUED to October 12, 2018 at 9:30 a.m. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =302/rbu

rulings.Case Title: GLORIA LEE ET AL VS. MEGABUS WEST, LLC, DBA MEGABUS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday September 26, 2018, Line 6, (part 1 of 2 tentative rulings) DEFENDANT DISCOVERY MANAGEMENT, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Discovery Management, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is DENIED. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether the accident was incident to Kennedy's employment duties and whether the use of a vehicle was an implied condition of Kennedy's employment. Kennedy was working the 7:30 p.m. to 4:00 a.m. porter shift on the date of the incident. (UMF 28.) Plaintiffs correctly note that state and federal law require employers to provide toilets or transportation to nearby toilets (29 CFR 1910.141(c)(1)(ii), seating (8 CCR 11090(14)(B), and a heated shelter where the temperature falls below 60 degrees Fahrenheit (8 CCR 11090(15)(B). Kennedy worked the porter station at 5th Street and Townsend St. (UMF 27, 29.) There are no facilities at that site; it merely has a bus stop sign. (Wong Depo., 82:1-22; 88:22-90:5.) Between midnight and 3:30 a.m., there is no scheduled activity at the Megabus stop and Kennedy was expected to "[j]ust be there." (Wong Depo., 89:24; Waters Depo., 54:20-55:6.) In Alma W. v. Oakland Unified School Dist. (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 133, 139, the court provides that: The determination as to whether an employee committed a tort during the course of his employment turns on whether or not: 1) the act performed was either required or 'incident to his duties' [citation], or 2) the employee's misconduct could be reasonably foreseen by the employer in any event [citation]." [citation] If an employee's actions fall within the range of actions covered by either part of this two-prong test, the employer will be liable for the wrong, even though the employee has acted maliciously and intentionally. In assessing whether an employee's wrongful act was required by or incidental to his duties, the law defines occupational duties broadly. The fact that an employee is not engaged in the ultimate object of his employment at the time of his wrongful act does not preclude attribution of liability to an employer. [citation] For example, acts necessary to the comfort, convenience, health, and welfare of the employee while at work, though strictly personal to himself and not acts of service, do not take him outside the scope of his employment.

rulings.Case Title: GLORIA LEE ET AL VS. MEGABUS WEST, LLC, DBA MEGABUS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Discovery Management, Inc. Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday September 26, 2018, Line 6, (part 2 of 2 tentative rulings) Defendant Discovery Management, Inc. Motion For Summary Judgment Here, there is a disputed issue of fact whether a porter's use of her car at the unsheltered, toiletless bus stop was incidental to the employment. Discovery further argues that even if use of a vehicle was an implied requirement, it is still entitled to summary judgment per the "going and coming rule." In Moradi v. Marsh USA, Inc. (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 886, 894-895, the court explains: [U]nder the 'going and coming' rule, employers are generally exempt from liability for tortious acts committed by employees while on their way to and from work because employees are said to be outside of the course and scope of employment during their daily commute." [citation] " 'The "going and coming" rule is sometimes ascribed to the theory that the employment relationship is "suspended" from the time the employee leaves [the workplace] until he returns ... or that in commuting he is not rendering service to his employer.' " [citation] " 'A well-known exception to the going-and-coming rule arises where the [employee's] use of [his or her own] car gives some incidental benefit to the employer. Thus, the key inquiry is whether there is an incidental benefit derived by the employer....' .... This exception to the going and coming rule ... has been referred to as the 'required-vehicle' exception.... The exception can apply if the use of a personally owned vehicle is either an express or implied condition of employment ..., or if the employee has agreed, expressly or implicitly, to make the vehicle available as an accommodation to the employer and the employer has 'reasonably come to rely upon its use and [to] expect the employee to make the vehicle available on a regular basis while still not requiring it as a condition of employment.' In this case, defendant argues that Kennedy was engaged in the purely personal act of driving to get lunch or taking her break at the time of the accident (around 10:30 p.m.). (UMF 26, 28, and 29.) The cited evidence (UMF 26) is speculative and fails to establish that fact. Based on the instant record, where Kennedy has yet to appear, it is equally possible that Kennedy was engaging in conduct that was actually furthering Discovery interests. As such, Discovery has not maintained its burden of production on this issue under CCP 437c. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: ALEXIS MASSOL VS. MINH GIAO KHAC LE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Plaintiff'S Motion For Leave To File Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday September 26, 2018, Line 11, PLAINTIFF ALEXIS MASSOL's Motion For Leave To File Second Amended Complaint Plaintiff Massol's motion for leave to file second amended complaint is GRANTED. There is a liberal policy in favor of amendment and defendants fail to demonstrate prejudice. The declaration of counsel Brophy indicates that defendants have yet to conduct discovery. Plaintiff's motion substantially complies with CRC 3.1324 and defendant's opposition to the motion on the merits waives any service defect. (See Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 690, 697.) Given the issues raised by the proposed amendments, the wiser course of conduct is to permit amendment and allow defendants to test the sufficiency of the new claims or allegations by demurrer or other appropriate proceedings. (See Cal. Casualty Gen. Ins. Co. v. Sup. Crt. 173 Cal.App.3d 274, 280-281.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/rbu

rulings.Case Title: JOSE GEREMIAS SALAZAR VS. DYNAMIC MOTORS INCORPORATED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice And Motion Compel Responses To Employment Law Form Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 26, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT DYNAMIC MOTORS, INC.'S Motion Compel Responses To Employment Law Form Interrogatories Judge Pro Tem Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOSE GEREMIAS SALAZAR VS. DYNAMIC MOTORS INCORPORATED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice To Compel Responses To Form Interrogatories-General
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 26, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT DYNAMIC MOTORS, INC.'S Motion To Compel Responses To Form Interrogatories-General. Judge Pro Tem Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Judge Pro Tem. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Judge Pro Tem will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.

rulings.Case Title: ANTOINETTTE ZIRKLE VS. JAMES BARNES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday September 26, 2018, Line 17, DEFENDANT JAMES BARNES, ADAM SPIEGELMAN's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT I rule on defendants James Barnes and Adam Spiegelman's motion to strike as follows: Punitive Damages: DENIED. The complaint adequately pleads bases for punitive damages. (See, e.g., 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 24, 29, 40.) Attorney Fees: DENIED. Plaintiff has served requests for admission and may be able seek attorney fees under CCP §2033.420(a). Civil Code §3345 Treble Damages: GRANTED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Plaintiff does not plead that she is disabled or 65 years of age or older. (See id.; Civ. Code §1761 (f&g).) Rescission: DENIED. An annuity contract is adequately pled. (Cmplt. 10-13.) Rescission is not necessarily stated as a cause of action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed judicial council order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/rbu

rulings.Case Title: ANTOINETTTE ZIRKLE VS. JAMES BARNES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday September 26, 2018, Line 17, DEFENDANTs JAMES BARNES and ADAM SPIEGELMAN's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendants James Barnes and Adam Spiegelman demur to the five causes of action in plaintiff's complaint. The demurrers are OVERRULED in their entirety: 1st (Negligence): The elements of negligence are adequately pled. The duty element is pled, at a minimum, by allegations that plaintiff had a long-term relationship with defendants as trusted financial advisers and planning specialists and that they claimed to be annuity experts. Defendants seem to claim to be mere insurance agents; that is a dispute to be adjudicated. Other arguments need not be reached, as a demurrer must dispose of an entire cause of action. (Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) §7.42.2.) 2nd (Business & Professions Code §17200): The demurrer assumes that the complaint only pleads unlawful business practices, but it also pleads unfair and fraudulent practices. 3rd & 4th (Fraudulent Concealment, Fraudulent Misrepresentation): The complaint pleads fraud with adequate specificity. (See, e.g., Cmplt. 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 24, 29, 40.) 5th (Breach of Fiduciary Duty): Plaintiff pleads that she had a long-term relationship with defendants as trusted financial advisers and planning specialists and that they claimed to be annuity experts. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed judicial council order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/rbu

rulings.Case Title: BAY AREA DIAMOND WHOLESALERS, INC. VS. C.H.A CORP ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday September 26, 2018, Line 18, DEFENDANTs C.H.A CORP and GIDEON CHAMDI's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendants demur to five causes of action in plaintiff's first amended complaint (FAC). The demurrers are OVERRULED in their entirety: 1st (Breach of Written Contract): The FAC pleads that its exhibit B combined with the parties' custom and practice to form a contract; no conflict appears on the FAC's face. 3rd (Specific Performance): Though technically a remedy, specific performance is often pled as a "cause of action;" Rogers v. Davis (1994) 28 Cal.App.4th 1215 did not find that practice demurrable. Specific performance and damages may be pled in the alternative. (Id. at 1220.) 7th (Business & Professions Code §17200): At a minimum, fraudulent business practices are pled. 8th (Fraud): At a minimum, deceit and false promise fraud are adequately pled. 9th (Conversion): Conversion is adequately pled. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/rbu
